title: All Your Favorite Gadgets Are Getting Way More Expensive … Again
gist: A fresh round of consumer-electronics price hikes (Apple, Xbox, Sony) is being driven chiefly by a memory-chip shortage caused by AI data-center demand, and the secondhand/refurbished market is booming as used phones appreciate rather than depreciate.

insights.claim: A new round of consumer-electronics price increases is being driven chiefly by a memory-chip shortage as component producers prioritize manufacturing chips for AI data centers over other tech.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: Memory-supply tightness driven by AI capex is a durable input-cost tailwind for pricing power at memory makers and a margin headwind for device OEMs.
insights.quote: the chief culprit is the ongoing memory shortage, i.e., software component producers making it a priority to manufacture chips for AI data centers over other tech that requires them
insights.locator: This fresh bout of price increases

insights.claim: Apple raised prices on MacBooks and iPads in June 2026, Xbox consoles get more expensive starting August, following already-raised prices on Sony's PlayStation 5 Pro.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: Broad simultaneous OEM price hikes signal industry-wide cost pass-through, not company-specific issues; watch device-maker gross margins.
insights.locator: In June, Apple announced increased prices

insights.claim: Companies would normally absorb a short-term component blip, but the memory shortage is expected to persist because the AI bubble has not popped, forcing sustained cost pass-through to consumers.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: Treat elevated device pricing as structural, not transitory; the shortage's duration is tethered to AI-capex cycle length.
insights.quote: If this were a short-term issue, the companies affected would likely have absorbed the costs. But the memory shortage does not seem to be disappearing anytime soon, and the AI bubble has not exactly popped yet.
insights.locator: This fresh bout of price increases

insights.claim: The Global Electronics Association's chief economist says 'waiting is not a strategy' for buyers because the shortage will persist for the foreseeable future.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: An industry economist forecasting no near-term relief is a signal that memory tightness is a multi-quarter theme.
insights.quote: Waiting is not a strategy right now and probably won't be for the foreseeable future.
insights.locator: In the past, you maybe could have waited out little blips

insights.claim: Device makers are described as very intentional and coordinated in pricing, and once a product has already taken a price increase buyers likely have time before the next one.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: Pricing moves are strategic and staggered; already-hiked SKUs may represent the near-term floor and pricing power is being actively managed.
insights.quote: They are very intentional in their pricing, and there's a lot of coordination that goes along with all of that
insights.locator: They are very intentional in their pricing

insights.claim: Fear of inflation is pushing consumers to upgrade devices more rapidly than they otherwise would, pulling demand forward.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: Inflation-driven pull-forward could inflate near-term device sales but borrow from future demand.
insights.quote: just pushing people to upgrade more rapidly because they're scared of inflation, basically
insights.locator: People are worried that they're going to have to pay

insights.claim: Used smartphones are selling for 10 to 20 percent more than in December 2025, reversing the normal pattern where used phones depreciate over time.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: Appreciating used-phone prices signal a booming recommerce cycle; a tradable tailwind for resale platforms like Back Market and B-Stock.
insights.quote: used smartphones are selling for 10 to 20 percent more than they were in December 2025
insights.locator: Sean Cleland, the vice president of Mobility tech

insights.claim: Even after the supply chain corrects, secondary-market device pricing is expected to settle a step above 2025 levels rather than fully reverting.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: A permanent re-rating of used-device value benefits recommerce economics beyond the acute shortage.
insights.quote: Secondary market pricing will go back to normal depreciation, but it will continue being a step above what it was in 2025. It just never comes all the way back.
insights.locator: The supply chain will correct eventually

insights.claim: Manufacturers are leaning into their own buyback and trade-in programs for phones, computers, tablets, watches, and headphones as trade-in values rise during the shortage.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: Higher trade-in values let OEMs subsidize new-device upgrades and capture resale margin; watch for expanded first-party resale programs.
insights.locator: Manufacturers have also leaned into their own resale programs
